description: Medical debt navigation: hospital charity care under IRS 501(r), itemized bill audits, No Surprises Act protections, post-2023 credit reporting changes, and negotiation strategies with specific thresholds and timelines.
sources: No Surprises Act, Consolidated Appropriations Act 2021, effective Jan 1 2022
CFPB medical debt credit reporting rule (2024, effective 2025)
HHS Hospital Price Transparency Rule, effective Jan 1 2021
CMS financial assistance guidance, MLN Matters
NCUA/CFPB debt collection rules, Reg F effective Nov 2021

triples.subject: IRS 501(r)
triples.object: nonprofit hospitals to have a Financial Assistance Policy (FAP), limit charges to FAP-eligible patients, and make reasonable efforts to determine FAP eligibility before collection

triples.subject: Financial Assistance Policy (FAP)
triples.object: eligibility criteria, basis for calculating amounts charged, method for applying, and plain-language summary — hospitals must post FAP on website and make available in ERs

triples.subject: FAP income threshold
triples.object: most hospitals set free care at 200–250% FPL and discounted care up to 350–400% FPL; thresholds vary by hospital — always ask for the hospital's specific FAP

triples.subject: 501(r) collections restriction
triples.object: extraordinary collection actions (ECAs) — lawsuits, liens, wage garnishment, credit bureau reporting — until at least 120 days after first bill and after making reasonable FAP determination effort

triples.subject: FAP application
triples.object: patient may apply for FAP up to 240 days after first post-discharge billing statement; hospital must suspend ECAs while application is pending

triples.subject: itemized bill
triples.object: you have the right to request a complete itemized bill; hospitals must provide it; common errors include duplicate charges, upcoding, unbundling, and charges for services not rendered

triples.subject: itemized bill audit
triples.object: duplicate line items, wrong diagnosis code (affects pricing), charges for canceled procedures, observation vs. inpatient misclassification, medications billed at retail not contract rate

triples.subject: hospital price transparency
triples.object: hospitals to publish machine-readable file of all standard charges and a consumer-friendly display of 300 shoppable services — use to benchmark your bill against posted rates

triples.subject: No Surprises Act
triples.object: surprise bills from out-of-network providers at in-network facilities (ERs, air ambulance, and non-emergency care where you had no choice of provider)

triples.subject: No Surprises Act
triples.object: in-network cost-sharing amount; patient cannot be billed more than in-network deductible/copay/coinsurance for covered surprise bill services

triples.subject: No Surprises Act
triples.object: if billed incorrectly, submit complaint to CMS at cms.gov/nosurprises; 60-day window from receipt of bill to initiate dispute; insurer must respond within 30 days

triples.subject: No Surprises Act
triples.object: ground ambulance (excluded from federal law as of 2025), out-of-network providers you chose knowingly and signed consent for, or services at out-of-network facilities you chose

triples.subject: medical debt credit reporting (post-2023)
triples.object: CFPB finalized rule in January 2025 removing medical debt from credit reports entirely; if in effect, medical debt cannot appear on consumer credit reports

triples.subject: medical debt credit reporting (post-2023)
triples.object: as of July 2022, all three major bureaus removed paid medical debt and medical debts under $500; as of March 2023, removed medical debt under 1 year old

triples.subject: medical debt credit reporting (post-2023)
triples.object: debt collector cannot report medical debt to credit bureaus until at least 1 year from date of first delinquency (up from 6 months for medical debt under CFPB guidance)

triples.subject: medical debt negotiation
triples.object: apply for FAP before negotiating — charity care can eliminate or dramatically reduce the bill; skip this and you may negotiate against a number that should be $0

triples.subject: medical debt negotiation
triples.object: ask for Medicare rate as a benchmark — hospitals typically receive 20–40% of billed charges from Medicare; this is a reasonable negotiation floor for uninsured patients

triples.subject: medical debt negotiation
triples.object: hospitals often accept 40–60% of billed charges as lump-sum settlement; document any settlement agreement in writing before paying

triples.subject: medical debt negotiation
triples.object: 501(r) hospitals must offer payment plans to FAP-eligible patients; many hospitals have interest-free plans up to 24 months — ask specifically, it is rarely volunteered

triples.subject: medical billing advocate
triples.object: professional who reviews and disputes medical bills; typically paid on contingency (percentage of savings); find through Alliance of Claims Assistance Professionals (claims.org)

triples.subject: state charity care laws
triples.object: some states (CA, IL, NJ, NY, TX) have charity care laws that go beyond 501(r) and cover for-profit hospitals or set higher income thresholds — check state health department

triples.subject: California AB 1320 (2022)
triples.object: hospitals to screen patients for charity care eligibility before reporting debt to collection or credit bureaus; extends charity care to 400% FPL minimum

triples.subject: medical debt lawsuit defense
triples.object: challenge whether hospital complied with 501(r) before filing suit — failure to make reasonable FAP eligibility effort is a violation that can void the collection action

triples.subject: medical debt lawsuit defense
triples.object: varies by state: typically 3–6 years for written contracts (medical bills); after SOL expires, debt is time-barred — collector cannot sue, but debt still technically exists

triples.subject: CMS complaint
triples.object: 1-800-MEDICARE or cms.gov for Medicare billing issues; use cms.gov/nosurprises for No Surprises Act violations; state insurance commissioner for insurance billing disputes

triples.subject: emergency care
triples.object: under EMTALA, hospitals with ERs must screen and stabilize any patient regardless of ability to pay — you cannot be turned away from an ER; billing comes after stabilization
